data.title: Maersk - Playing The Long Game
data.description:
Portfolio Commentary

As of 10/1/2025, my cumulative return for my portfolio from inception is 115% and overall return year-to date is 13.2%.

Here is my current allocation as of 10/1/2025:

*This does not indicate other holdings are Buys in my opinion*

Since my last article in May 2025, I sold my Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and American Express (AXP) positions to add more Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) and Bill Holdings (BILL). AXP at a 102% gain and Berkshire at a 35% gain.

(SPY) is up ~12% YTD. My portfolio is up ~15% YTD return.

Not many active managers are outperforming US indexes this year. And if they are outperforming US indexes, they are probably buying very over-valued companies.

My objective is to have gains of 15% every year and right now my annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) is 32% a year since inception.

I first invested in Maersk ADR shares in September 2024 when shares were below $8/share. I purchased many more shares in January, making Maersk 50% of my portfolio. My average cost for (OTCPK:AMKBY) is now $8.55.

The article below discusses one of my personal investments. I am not writing this because I am recommending you should buy the stock without further due diligence. Investing in single stocks is very risky. I recommend to everyone to buy SPY if you don’t want a financial advisor but want to invest in the stock market.

Maersk Overview

AP Moller-Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) was founded in Denmark in 1904, making it over 120 years old. Their core business is the Ocean segment (container shipping). The other two segments are Terminals (operating port infrastructure) and Logistics & Services (air freight, trucking/last mile delivery, and warehousing). Maersk is increasingly focused on becoming a global shipper of container logistics, aiming to manage the entire supply chain rather than just the ocean leg.

Before Covid, shipping was a cyclical business with modest returns. Maersk typically earned around $2-3 billion/year pre-pandemic.

Then Covid happened and bottlenecked the whole supply chain and the shipping companies made large amounts of money. Larger than usual.

Maersk generated a Net Income of $17.9 billion in 2021 and $29.2 billion in 2022—totaling over $47 billion in profit in just two years.

Now that spot rates have normalized profitability has compressed significantly. Maersk continues to be profitable through most rate environments.

Maersk trades for ~$30 billion today.

Maersk trades at 10x times or 10% yield 2018/2019 net income.

Maersk trades at 5x times, or 20% yield, 2024 net income.

Global Ocean Shipping Outlook

Many publicly-traded ocean freight companies are trading at or beneath the replacement value of their assets. This valuation implies the market believes these companies will lose money for the foreseeable future.

But who is going to ship goods across the seas if these companies fail? According to the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), approximately 80% to 90% of global trade by volume is transported by sea. It is the circulatory system of the global economy. Is there a different or cheaper way to transport millions of tons of goods to North America or Europe? The answer is no. Similar to railroads within the United States, containerships are the cheapest mode of transportation (by weight) across oceans.

The biggest narrative keeping the ocean shipping sector trading at single-digit PEs and low price-to-book ratios is the fear of massive oversupply and a subsequent collapse in spot rates.

This fear is not unfounded. During the COVID-19 pandemic, shipping companies generated unprecedented profits and reinvested heavily in new ships. This has resulted in a record-breaking "order book" (the pipeline of ships currently under construction). As of August 2025, the container ship orderbook reached a record high of 10.4 million TEU (twenty-foot equivalent units).

As shown in the chart below, Maersk is the second largest liner in the world by capacity/TEUs.

Maersk is the second-largest carrier in the world and the largest publicly traded container shipping company by capacity. This scale is crucial in a commoditized, high-fixed-cost industry.

Analysts are concerned that with more supply coming, demand will fall.

Current Rate Environment

As seen in the Containerized Freight Index above, ocean freight rates are continuing to fall. This index tracks the most popular trade lanes in ocean shipping and aggregates the rates into one figure. As you can see, thanks to COVID shutdowns in 2020, freight rates boomed to all time highs. As of late September 2025, rates are falling to 2 year lows and approaching pre-Covid levels.

Just as analysts projected, rates are declining.

This has lead to the recent price decline in Maersk's stock price as Maersk's profitability is directly impacted by freight rates.

Now that freight rates are at breakeven levels (or even below breakeven for some), what does that mean?

It's likely that most, if not all, ocean shipping companies will lose money in the short term as the price to ship a container falls below their cost.

And the larger players in the space most likely survive.

If you are the low-cost leader and well-capitalized, you should survive any downturn and gain market share.

The market is pricing in the worst-case scenario while ignoring the strategic shifts Maersk has made to navigate this environment.

Ocean Strategy: Reliability + Cost Leader with High Volume

In February 2025, Maersk launched the "Gemini Cooperation," a partnership with Hapag-Lloyd (the world's 5th largest carrier). The explicit goal of this alliance is to achieve schedule reliability above 90%. This is revolutionary in an industry where the norm, according to maritime data provider Sea-Intelligence, typically hovers around 50-60%.

The network uses large, efficient vessels on mainline routes that sail only between key transshipment hubs. Crucially, these hubs are largely owned or controlled by Maersk (through APM Terminals) or Hapag-Lloyd.

The results are already proving the concept. During the Q2 2025 earnings call, CEO Vincent Clerc reported that the network was achieving +90% reliability.

This partnership allows Maersk to pursue a high-volume, cost-leader strategy.

In Q2 2025, Maersk charged lower prices per TEU compared to major competitors. The Gemini Network increased their volume delivered.

When compared to these other publicly traded liners, in Q2 2025, Maersk had the highest volume at the lowest price.

This strategy is designed to gain market share by increasing capacity without buying more ships. During a downturn, offering a superior product at a competitive price should prove successful.

Contracts vs. Spot Revenue

Maersk has two buckets for bookings: spot (rates agreed upon at the time of transaction) and contract (rates locked in 6+ months in advance). Now management refers to these buckets as short-term vs long-term products.

Contract revenue uses future contracts traded in the market. This means today, 10/1/2025, there is a futures contract for one TEU delivery from China to the US West Coast for 12/1/2026 or 2/1/2026.

Offering customers long-term contracts provides the customer (like Walmart, Nike, or Home Depot) peace of mind against spot rate volatility and ensures Maersk maintains a base load of volume. When spot rates decline, contract revenue provides a buffer. The downside is that if spot rates rapidly increase, Maersk leaves money on the table.

Maersk has heavily favored this conservative approach in the past. In their most recent interim report (Q2 2025), they shared that their revenue was split 50/50 between contracts and spot.

Currently, all the freight futures for 2026 deliveries are above current spot rates.

Meaning, if Maersk leans more into long-term contracts, they will make more revenue than shipping companies delivering more at spot rates.

Another advantage for the large-volume player.

APM Terminals

Another aspect of Maersk that may not be appreciated by the market is their investments into ocean terminals.

Maersk has invested billions into port terminals that load and off-load containers from Maersk ships or competitors' ships. With the establishment of the Gemini Network, APM Terminal's revenue and profits continue to grow at a steady pace thanks to the higher volume strategy in the Ocean segment.

Last 12 months, the Terminals segment had earnings of $1.5B. Growing revenue 15-20% a year with $1.5B profit.

I could make the argument that the APM Terminals segment alone is worth $30B. And Maersk as a whole is trading for $30B.

$1.5 billion x 20 times multiple = $30 billion valuation

A Fortress Balance Sheet

The market is currently valuing Maersk around a $30 billion market cap as of September 2025.

Maersk has $88B of assets and $30 billion in TOTAL liabilities.

Book value, or replacement value, is what accounting says it would cost to recreate Maersk today—to build the modernized fleet, secure the highly coveted global terminal slots, and replicate their logistics network. Book value is almost double the market cap of Maersk shares.

As of the Q2 2025 report, Maersk reported net interest bearing debt of -$2.5 billion. This means Maersk is in a strong net cash position—they have cash of $19 billion and debt of $17 billion.

Most asset heavy businesses have high debt positions, like railroads, because of large amounts of fixed assets (ships, terminals, etc.). Maersk has $88 billion in assets.

The book value per share is ~DKK 23,000, while the stock is trading near DKK 12,500. It's almost like buying a dollar for 50 cents.

This is a margin of safety. If the company is unprofitable in the short-term, at least you bought the company at a price beneath Net Asset Value.

Share Buybacks

In February 2025, Maersk initiated a significant DKK 14.4 billion (~$2 billion USD) share buyback program.

Since the initiation, Maersk has bought 5.5% of $Maersk-B shares outstanding. That's a 5% yield to shareholders in half a year.

Then you factor in the price support.

Maersk releases weekly press releases announcing how many shares they bought in the week prior. This is not done by typical NYSE/American stocks.

These buybacks are making up a significant portion of the daily traded volume—often 15-25% of shares being sold—nearing the regulatory maximum.

The EU Commission and the SEC have laws against companies purchasing more than 25% of daily traded volume because companies could manipulate stock prices with these large purchases.

If Apple (AAPL) decided to buy 25% of their average daily traded volume, they would have enough cash to do it for ~18 days. This is the difference between a highly liquid stock and low liquidity stock like Maersk.

Because Maersk shares have little activity/selling, Maersk is approaching this "stock manipulation" threshold while trying to return only $2B of capital to shareholders over the course of a year.

The stock repurchase plan provides strong support for the share price and reduces the share count at prices well below book value...

With a net cash position, it's likely that Maersk announces another stock repurchase plan in February 2026.

Risks

No investment is without risk, and Maersk faces several significant challenges. Most of which have already been discussed.

Oversupply and Rate Collapse: This is the primary risk. If the delivery of the 10.4 million TEU order book overwhelms global demand, spot rates could fall below cash break-even levels for a prolonged period in which Maersk will be unprofitable or breakeven.

Normalization of Red Sea Routes: Currently, disruptions in the Red Sea are forcing ships to sail around the Cape of Good Hope. This longer route effectively absorbs excess capacity (estimated by analysts to be 7-10% of global capacity). If the Red Sea routes reopen safely, this capacity will be released back into the market, putting immediate downward pressure on rates.

Global Recession: Ocean freight is highly correlated with global GDP. A significant economic downturn would reduce the demand for goods, compounding the oversupply issue.

DKK currency risk: I invest in Maersk through the ADR (OTCPK:AMKBY). This means currency exchange rates between US dollars and Danish Krones affect the ADR share price. Since my initial investment, this has been a tailwind providing more gains. If the dollar strengthens more in the future, the ADR will be negatively affected. If the dollar continues to weaken, the ADR will be positively affected.

Conclusion

My thesis is that the market is overly focused on the near-term supply headwinds in the ocean shipping sector and underappreciating Maersk's strategic pivot towards reliability.

The ocean freight industry may have too many ships in the short term, but Maersk is exceptionally well-positioned to survive falling spot rates due to its strong balance sheet, contract-heavy revenue mix, and the efficiency and volume of the Gemini Cooperation.

In an industry becoming more commoditized, Maersk's strategy of providing low-cost leadership combined with premium reliability should increase market share and ensure long-term profitability. The current valuation provides a significant margin of safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Accenture: Short-Term Challenges, Long-Term Potential
data.description:
There is a huge difference between how we're all using AI in our individual lives that is incredibly easy and what it takes to use it in an enterprise.

The quote belongs to Julie Sweet, Accenture's (NYSE:ACN) CEO, and she said it during the conference call where they presented the Q4 and FY25 results. Sweet took a few minutes to explain in detail the way in which the company is expanding its revenue through customer demand to incorporate greater uses of AI across the business organization. In this context, she said the following, and I think it's wonderful because it also summarizes my impression of AI: it is a revolutionary technology, but generating profits above the invested capital isn't something as quick or simple as it may seem.

Similarly, Sweet highlighted the well-known topic of ROI, a topic recently addressed by MIT, which noted that only 5% reported achieving positive returns on investment.

It is well recognized that advanced AI has taken the mind share of CEOs, the C-suite and Boards faster than any technology development we've seen in the past 2 decades. At the same time, as reported widely, value realization has been underwhelming for many and enterprise adoption at scale is slow other than with digital natives. This is why our clients are turning to us. We know that the gap between mind share and faster actual adoption is because the enterprise reinvention required to truly unlock the value of advanced AI is hard and has significant costs.

Julie Sweet, CEO of Accenture

Investment Thesis

I wanted to start my analysis of ACN with this perspective from Sweet, which I found very interesting, regarding a current dilemma that some analysts, usually labeled as pessimistic, have been warning about. Wall Street is racing at a speed that can't withstand the entire race for mass euphoria over AI. Ultimately, AI's leadership will be seen in the long term, since, as Sweet said, we are in the first stage.

On July 18th, I wrote an article about ACN and its AI potential, rating it a buy after the company suffered a negative impact due to the DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) cuts and a 6% year-over-year decline in new booking revenue.

Interestingly, now that the company has regained momentum in new deals and posted a 6% YoY increase (from $20.15 billion to $21.31 billion) in Q4 FY25, the bearish blow came from a slightly lower-than-market-expected earnings projection for fiscal year 2026. The share price fell from $239 to $230 after the results were released, although it rebounded to $247 in the last week. Looking at the bigger picture, that is, looking at the year-to-date figures, ACN didn't have the best performance seen in previous years and was somewhat left behind compared to the big tech companies that are dominating the market: it fell almost 30%.

Be careful; if we expand the perspective a little further, for example, to 5 years, the yield is not the most attractive, with a total return for investors of 18%. Moreover, the recent lows in August and September have not been seen since March 2021.

Anyway, I think that ACN is playing a different game since the AI boom that started to explode strongly in 2022. I'm confident that ACN has several key strategies that have led them to their great potential. I will mention three, but there could be more:

  • Procurement policy: According to a report (slide 121) by the European Commission, ACN leads global acquisition activity with an average of 9.1 operations per year, above Microsoft's (MSFT) 6.7 and IBM's 6.1. Recently, the company acquired CyberCX, an Australian cybersecurity brand; IAMConcepts, a Canadian identity security firm; and Orlade, a French advisory services company.

  • Leading partners: ACN is working with the major companies that provide cloud services (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Oracle, etc.), and these account for 60% of their revenue.
  • Staff training: Based on what was said on the conference call, in fiscal year 2023 the company had 40,000 AI professionals and currently has 77,000, accounting for more than 6,000 advanced AI projects by 2025.

ACN has been investing in AI since 2019, and in recent years, we can see, in my opinion, that ACN has gained the potential to be worth more than it currently is on the market, moving toward a P/E ratio of 19x compared to the sector median of 30x or the historical average of 29x over the last five years. This implies an undervaluation of approximately 34% to 36%.

In my last article, I focused more on ACN's leadership in the AI sphere. But today I want to analyze in more detail, in addition to the FY25 results, the reason for a further drop in price and its earnings projection in parallel with the market outlook, concluding that I not only maintain my Buy rating (the price has fallen 12% since the publication of my previous article), but I also confirm that ACN is a great future opportunity despite the present risks.

Q4 FY25 and the Positive: Recovery of New Bookings

The news of the new contracts eased the bearish sentiment in a context where expectations about AI-generated revenue are the most closely watched issue among investors. In the quarter, new contracts totaled $21.31 billion, while in the fiscal year they totaled $80.62 billion, a slight 1% contraction compared to $81.23 billion in FY2024. The significant bearish sentiment weighing on the company in 2025, partly due to federal spending cuts, ultimately led to a slight decline of just 1%. At the same time, GenAI's bookings were also encouraging: they were almost double those of 2024, at $5.9 billion.

The other metrics, in general, are all positive and exceeded market consensus figures, such as the revenue of $17.6 billion (7% YoY), $236 million above expectations, or the adjusted EPS of $3.03, a surprise of $0.06 compared to the estimated EPS.

If we leave aside the quarterly results and focus on the fiscal year, we will also find positive numbers, generally speaking, despite the headwinds: revenues were $69.7 billion (+7% YoY), with the largest increase in the financial services sector (+10%), the adjusted operating margin was 15.6% (+10 bps), and annual net income closed at $7.8 billion, above the $7.4 billion expected in 2024.

Another example of the company's resilience is, in my view, the best performance of the year, which, in my opinion, is the free cash flow of $10.9 billion (+26% YoY), which allowed it to return $8.3 billion to shareholders, of which $4.6 billion went to share repurchases and the remainder to dividend payments, a solid and consistent cash flow that demonstrates the company's strong position.

FY25 and the Challenges: What's Causing the Decline?

We've already mentioned the cuts at AFS (Accenture Federal Services), and it's estimated there will still be some impact on revenue during 2026, as the company estimates growth of between 2% and 5%, assuming a 1% to 1.5% impact on AFS. Excluding the cuts at AFS, revenue profitability is close to the fiscal year 2025 yield of 7%, with a target of 6% at the most optimistic end.

Although it may seem like a logical doubt for many investors who view the 2026 estimate as a concern, the reality, from my perspective, is that this is a long-term opportunity because 1) there are no operational issues at the company and 2) the pessimism made the stock a cheap and attractive entry option. Furthermore, all other forecasts are solid, in my opinion, with the added benefit that shareholder returns continue to grow, according to estimates, to $9.3 billion.

On the other hand, there are other areas where the company faces challenges, one of which is employee training and talent turnover to optimize AI development. In this regard, the company reported a $615 million cost for business optimization, of which $344 million is for employee severance pay, reflected in the decrease in headcount, which fell from 790,000 in the previous quarter to 779,000. According to statements made during the call by CFO Angie Park, they plan to add an additional $250 million by Q1FY26 (Nov 2025), completing the process at a total cost of $865 million.

Another positive outlook for the future, rather than a challenge, is the steady growth of the company's investment in cybersecurity, one of the largest revenue drivers in recent years, especially thanks to the various acquisitions of smaller companies. Security is the fastest-growing job type in local currency in FY25, up 16% to $10 billion, with an estimated 9% CAGR through 2028.

Valuation

In my last article on the company, I developed a valuation model using multiples compared to the sector. However, this time, to understand the company's value relative to its earnings per share and the market outlook, I will perform a valuation based on GAAP EPS and GAAP P/E, metrics that are more conservative than non-GAAP and which I consider the basis of my calculations.

I want to clarify that I aim to be conservative to demonstrate that ACN only needs a modest amount of confidence to recover its recent historical prices. In this pursuit of conservatism, I am using the lower end of the GAAP EPS projection for FY2026, which is $13.19. I could use the midpoint estimate, $13.38, or even the upper end, $13.57, but I am opting for a conservative model.

I apply the same concept to the expected GAAP P/E, which could be 30x or even higher in an optimistic scenario. Below, I show the evolution of the P/E over the last 5 years.

Based, then, on a GAAP EPS of $13.19, I estimate conservative scenarios with a recovery of the GAAP P/E that, without reaching the historical average, would still generate attractive returns. The base price I use is $245.

Scenario EPS GAAP Estimate P/E GAAP Target Price Upside
Bearish $13.19 18x $237 -3.2%
Base $13.19 20x $264 +7.8%
Moderate $13.19 23x $303 +23.6%
Conservative $13.19 25x $329 +34.3%
Mildly bullish $13.19 27x $356 +45.1%

Pessimistic valuation scenarios, i.e., GAAP P/E below 18x, haven't been seen in the last 5 years, and we'd have to go back to 2017 to find a 16x ratio. That's why I used a floor of 18x, so I don't see much further downside. All other scenarios are bullish but moderate, and I consider the 25x scenario the most likely within a conservative scenario. In that case, the upside is 34.3%, and the target price is $329. I reiterate that I'm also using the lower estimate of GAAP EPS, and there's still room for upside.

Risks

The company's main risks are largely reflected in the challenges outlined throughout the article, although there are obviously other external risks that could end up impacting the outlook for a recovery in the stock price. Among these risks is US macroeconomic uncertainty, which, while pointing to activity growth for 2026, inflation and employment data are not looking very good in the eyes of the Federal Reserve. The recent rate cut and the projections of two more cuts by 2025 clear some doubts about activity, although it raises other concerns about inflation control.

On the other hand, ACN is also heavily exposed to international markets, as EMEA and Asia Pacific represent almost 50% of its revenue, which also entails foreign exchange risks. Added to this is the uncertainty surrounding potential labor restrictions on foreigners by the federal government, such as the H-1B visa measure and the $100,000 tariff. Sweet explained on the call that this will not have an impact on the company since only 5% of its staff has H-1B visas. ACN's largest concentration of staff is in India, with around 350,000 employees. By this, I mean that any new labor market measure that leads to cost overruns poses a risk to ACN under the Trump administration.

Conclusion

In short, the current challenges are convincing the market that the current value is a bargain compared to its potential and dispelling all shadows of future federal spending cuts. The opportunities, meanwhile, lie in the company's operational capabilities and its AI adoption strategy for its corporate clients. The added benefit is that the upside potential is high, and, in my opinion, this is a long-term investment.

data.title: DIAX: Merger On The Table, The Discount To NAV Could Shrink
data.description:
Thesis

We have covered the Nuveen Dow 30 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (DIAX) several times in the past on the Seeking Alpha platform. The name is a buy-write CEF from Nuveen that has poor structuring and poor long-term results. Our issue with DIAX has always been around its Dow Jones to S&P 500 basis, a structural item that we highlighted in our last article on the website.

Nuveen seems to be undergoing a streamlining of operations in the CEF space, with the recent merger of Nuveen Preferred Securities & Income Opportunities Fund (JPI) into the Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC), which was completed on September 22, 2025.

It seems like now the fund manager is looking at doing the same streamlining when it comes to its buy-write closed-end funds. In today's article, we are going to discuss the most recent corporate action and our view on the proposal.

What is happening?

The Board of Directors for several Nuveen CEFs has approved a potential corporate action:

CHICAGO, September 19, 2025--(BUSINESS WIRE)--The Boards of Trustees of Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX), Nuveen Dow 30℠ Dynamic Overwrite Fund (NYSE:DIAX), and Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (SPXX) have approved a proposal to merge the funds. The proposed mergers, if approved by shareholders, would combine BXMX and DIAX into SPXX. The mergers are intended to create a larger fund with lower net operating expenses and increased trading volume on the exchange for common shares.

Any merger in the CEF space starts with the Board of Trustees (Board of Directors). This approval starts the process, with the shareholders having the final say in the mergers.

The next step is for each CEF shareholders to vote on the proposed corporate action, with the outcome contingent on such a vote.

The Nuveen CEFs have not been the brightest stars

We have covered all the respective CEFs on the Seeking Alpha platform in the past, with the funds not being the brightest stars in the buy-write arena:

  • DIAX: The Basis Is Still There, Keep Avoiding This Buy-Write CEF.
  • BXMX: A Disappointing 2024 For This Equity CEF.
  • SPXX: Much Better Choices Out There.

The market has taken a similar view on the CEFs via the discount to NAV metric:

All the respective CEFs are trading at very deep discounts to NAV, all close to -10%. There is a whole new generation of ETFs and CEFs in the buy-write arena that have attracted significant capital and interest via their robust performance. Not for the Nuveen funds:

Please note that the Nuveen CEFs are at the bottom, all severely underperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) and other competitors in the space. In each dedicated article, we discussed the structural issues with each fund; thus, the above does not come as a surprise.

Our view on the corporate action

We are of the opinion that a consolidation is beneficial to the DIAX shareholders because it would remove the basis, although the fund mandate might change to follow the S&P 500. The asset manager is clearly trying to optimize and refocus the equities management team, and having one larger flagship fund is a better way to do it. Without any changes, DIAX will continue to underperform and be a forgotten CEF at a large discount to NAV. Posting a +39% total return when the SPX is up over +93% is not acceptable in our view.

We can see newcomers in the space like the Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) garnering very significant interest and AUM due to their performance:

The market has already voted, and it has moved toward top performers who care about the underlying structure and relative returns versus an index. As a reminder, one would get an 11% yield via SPYI, and its performance in the past year is as follows:

While it is not entirely correct to benchmark DIAX to SPYI since DIAX is supposed to follow the Dow Jones, we can see the fund's significant lag to the Dow index itself in the above.

At the end of the day, investors are chasing performance in the buy-write space, and laggards will not attract AUM or interest. In fact, laggards end up trading at large discounts to NAV.

Closing of the discount to NAV—a potential +10% gain

A corporate action that would result in a larger, more streamlined fund with better structuring would probably also narrow down the discount to NAV. Currently, DIAX has a 9.6% discount to NAV, which could theoretically move to flat to NAV. We have seen these types of moves in the past when weaker funds were merged into better-structured funds. While nobody is guaranteeing any narrowing, a larger, more focused fund that would target better performance metrics could entice market participants to close some discount gap.

If the new entity performs better than its cohort or at least in line with peers, the market will reward it with a narrower discount. Thus, voting in favor of this corporate action can theoretically help performance via a lower discount to net asset value.

Conclusion

DIAX is a buy-write fund from Nuveen. The CEF has been plagued by structural issues and has underperformed. The market has 'rewarded' DIAX with a -10% discount to NAV, highlighting its issues. Nuveen is looking to streamline its buy-write CEF business via a merger that would see DIAX, SPXX, and BXMX combine. We view this as a positive outcome for the DIAX shareholders, which could see the massive -9% discount to NAV narrow to almost flat. We are in favor of the merger.

data.title: Howmet Aerospace: A High-Growth Investment In The Resilient Aerospace Sector
data.description:
Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM), a leading player in the aerospace industry, is one of the top-performing stocks this year, driven by secular growth trends in airplane spare parts. It is up 78.03% year-to-date, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 13% rise.

After the stock's spectacular rise this year and elevated valuation, some might wonder whether the stock is worthy of an investment today. Investors bullish on the company's position in several high-growth markets might say yes. The company provides Original Equipment Manufacturer ("OEM") and spare parts for jet engines in the commercial and defense aerospace industry. What some may not know is that companies utilize jet engines beyond the aerospace industry. I only discovered that years ago when I worked as an engineer on a gas turbine ship. When I took classes on how to operate a gas turbine on an oceangoing vessel, that's when I discovered that gas turbines are modified jet engines. So, Howmet also manufactures parts used in Industrial Gas Turbines [IGT] for electrical utilities, maritime, and oil & gas industries. Despite its focus on IGT and jet engines, the company also manufactures parts outside of these areas. For instance, it has a Forged Wheels segment for commercial transportation (trucks) that manufactures forged aluminum wheels.

The company derives most of its revenue from selling parts directly to OEMs, which are manufacturers such as GE Aerospace (GE), that in turn use those products as components in their final, end-user products (jet engines or gas turbines). Although OEM sales for new aircraft are the most significant part of Howmet's business, they carry lower margins due to the way the aeronautical industry structures OEM and spare part sales.

OEMs have significant bargaining power in component sales for new jet engines. The OEMs offer component makers a massive, guaranteed volume of part sales over the entire life of manufacturing a specific engine. For component manufacturers to receive this guaranteed volume, OEMs ask for a significant volume discount (often slightly above cost) for new engine parts. In return for the volume discount and lower initial OEM margin, the component manufacturer locks in a position as the sole-source supplier for the engine (a virtual monopoly), guaranteeing higher-margin aftermarket (spare part) sales. Since the maintenance schedule requires operators to replace critical parts over a specific period, spare parts revenue becomes recurring.

As a result, the company's most significant growth engine is its Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) business, also known as its spare parts business, which generates a growing, high-margin, and recurring revenue stream. Investors like recurring revenue because it's predictable. The market tends to raise the valuation of companies with growing recurring revenue streams.

On the second-quarter 2025 earnings call, Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") John C. Plant said, "Spares for commercial aerospace, defense aerospace, and IGT/industrial have increased by some 40% year-over-year and were at 20% of total revenue." Spares accounted for only 11% of total revenue in 2019. Therefore, the company's spare parts business increased by nine points over the last six years. As long as its spare parts continue growing, investors will have a bias towards raising the stock's valuation. Therefore, I consider the stock a buy for long-term investors seeking growth.

Strong Secular Growth in Aerospace Spares and MRO

Plane manufacturers such as The Boeing Company (BA) and Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF, OTCPK:EADSY) have been unable to meet the post-COVID demand for airplanes due to supply chain disruptions caused by the pandemic. Brookfield Aviation, an aviation services company, published an article in early January that stated:

Aircraft deliveries have dropped sharply from 1,813 units in 2018 to an estimated 1,254 in 2024 30% below projections. Although deliveries are forecast to rise to 1,802 units in 2025, this remains far below the 2,293 initially expected.

As a result, the global fleet of airplanes is aging. Since older planes may require more frequent and more parts replacement, Howmet's MRO (spare parts) business directly benefits from the plane manufacturers' inability to produce more planes. How long can Howmet benefit from this trend? At a recent Jeffries Conference, CEO Plant indicated that this trend could last until at least 2028. Specifically, he said at the Jeffries Conference:

If you use CFM [CFM International] as a proxy for that [spare parts] discussion, previously, people used to talk about it peaking in 2024 or 2025. And a year or so or maybe 2 years ago saying, I think it's more likely going to be 2028. And I think now everybody is coalescing around volumes are going to continue to increase through 2028 and then degrade very slowly from that point. But meanwhile, all the, I'll say, more modern engines, given the achieved duty cycles of those engines, those are coming in more frequently for spares and turbine blade replacements.

Plant likely uses CFM as a proxy because Howmet is a significant supplier to CFM, which is considered the largest commercial aircraft engine manufacturer in the world, based on the number of engines produced and the size of its installed fleet. If you are like me, you might have thought GE Aerospace was the largest jet engine manufacturer, and you may not have heard of CFM International. Technically, both GE Aerospace and CFM International are the largest aircraft engine manufacturers because CFM International is a 50/50 joint venture between GE Aerospace (U.S.) and Safran SA (OTCPK:SAFRF, OTCPK:SAFRY) (France), formed in 1974. CFM manufactures the legacy CFM56-5B engine family that winds up mostly in Airbus planes and the CFM56-7B engine family that winds up mostly in legacy Boeing 737 planes.

Currently, CFM is winding down its legacy engines in favor of selling its next-generation LEAP (Leading Edge Aviation Propulsion) Series: LEAP-1A (for the A320neo Family), LEAP-1B (for the Boeing 737 MAX Family), and LEAP-1C (for the COMAC C919). CFM first introduced the LEAP engine to the market in 2016.

Among the reasons the industry believes spare parts sales will peak in 2028 is a combination of the LEAP engine becoming the predominant engine in the late 2020s and airlines scheduling the retirement of older planes with legacy engines. Right now, some of CFM's earlier sales of the LEAP Engine need earlier-than-planned maintenance due to durability issues when operating in hot climates and high concentrations of dust or sand (like the Middle East and India). These are issues that CFM aims to resolve in the future. However, these earlier-than-planned maintenance periods are currently sustaining robust sales of spare parts. As the company eventually resolves these LEAP engine issues, it expects LEAP to require longer periods between maintenance needs compared to legacy engines. Consequently, Howmet expects spare parts sales to eventually peak, particularly as airplane operators retire more legacy engines from service.

At this point, you might wonder whether Howmet will remain a worthwhile investment beyond 2028, given the potential declining demand for jet engine maintenance. Although the industry expects a more extended time between maintenance periods and fewer maintenance events with LEAP engines, which may initially seem detrimental to Howmet's spare parts business, several factors could benefit the company over the long term.

Jet engine manufacturers, such as CFM, establish a predetermined number of operating cycles, hours, or landings, or a specific date, for plane operators to replace critical components in an engine to prevent catastrophic failures. The industry refers to these vital components as Life-Limited Parts (LLPs). Although the LEAP engine reduces the need for more frequent minor repairs and maintenance, the need for mandatory replacement of LLPs remains.

The LEAP engine program is the fastest-ramping engine program in history, creating a massive install base. Once the install base comes up on the schedule for a significant overhaul to replace LLPs, the sheer volume of engines requiring LLP replacement simultaneously will create an unprecedented, massive wave of demand for Howmet's parts. The best part is that this spike in demand is predictable. I imagine that management will incorporate these demand spikes into its guidance, which should serve as catalysts for raising the company's valuation in the period beyond 2028. So, although the spare parts business may decline in 2028, the LLP replacement cycle means that its spare parts business should continue to rise in the long term in dramatic spurts.

Another significant benefit is that Howmet's parts in the LEAP engine have a higher average selling price ("ASP") than in legacy engines due to far more advanced, high-temperature, high-performance alloys and manufacturing processes. As a result, the spare parts in an LLP replacement cycle carry much higher margins compared to a maintenance cycle of a legacy engine.

Lastly, Howmet faces far fewer certified suppliers in the spare parts market for LEAP engines than it does in the legacy CFM56 engine market. When an engine market matures, more companies and third-party repair shops enter the Used Serviceable Material (USM) market, which involves removing parts from retired aircraft, inspecting, refurbishing, and certifying them to meet airworthiness regulations for reuse. Howmet faces significant competition from USM providers in the CFM56 engine market. However, since LEAP engines are so new, plane manufacturers have retired very few, if any, engines, so Howmet almost has a monopoly status selling LEAP engine LLP parts, which should allow it to maintain high margins for years until the USM market fully develops for LEAP engines.

Valuation

Howmet's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio of 10.30, much higher than its three-, five-, and seven-year medians, signals that either the market overvalues the stock, the market expects revenue to increase rapidly, or the quality (higher margin or certainty) of the revenue will increase. If the stock were to trade at its three-year median price today, its value would be $77.62, representing a 60% decline from its September 30 closing price of $196.23.

The following chart shows that analysts forecast Howmet to grow its EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) by a two-year compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 13.17%, from $2.347 billion in 2025 to $3.025 billion in 2027.

I use YCharts to derive figures for calculating a two-year CAGR, EV-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) valuation, and EBITDA margin to compare Howmet to its aviation industry peers, as shown in the table below. Based on the comparison, Howmet may deserve the highest EV/EBITDA valuation. However, there are two anomalies in this comparison.

Company name Forward EV/EBITDA Two-year EBITDA CAGR TTM EBITDA margin
Howmet 34.22 13.17% 28.07%
GE Aerospace 32.73 11.88% 21.23%
RTX Corporation (RTX) 21.21 8.03% 14.65%
TransDigm Group (TDG) 20.13 11.88% 51.14%
Hexcel Corp. (HXL) 16.49 20.34% 17.31%
ATI Inc. (ATI) 15.15 13.01% 17.00%
Lockheed Martin (LMT) 14.76 11.54% 10.45%
Textron (TXT) 10.00 7.13% 8.68%

Hexcel is the first significant anomaly in the above table, as it has the highest two-year EBITDA CAGR but a much lower forward EV/EBITDA than Howmet. This discrepancy doesn't necessarily mean that the market overvalues Howmet. First, Hexcel is coming off a significantly lower EBITDA estimate base ($342.31 million) compared to Howmet ($2.347 billion).

The market likely awards Howmet a higher forward EV/EBITDA of 34.22, based on its higher EBITDA margin and a more stable EBITDA base, which makes it harder for Howmet to post a higher CAGR than Hexcel. However, the quality of Howmet's EBITDA also makes its current estimated EBITDA growth rates more highly valued.

The second anomaly is TransDigm, which has a significantly higher EBITDA margin than Howmet but a much lower EV/EBITDA ratio. This discrepancy has occurred because TransDigm carries a considerably higher financial risk due to its substantial debt load. The following chart shows that TransDigm has a debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio of -5.01, indicating negative shareholders' equity, which may deter some from investing. On the other hand, Howmet's D/E of 0.68 is a sign of a healthy financial structure.

TransDigm also has a TTM debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.767, which some investors consider a sign of financial distress. In comparison, Howmet's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 1.639. The market generally considers a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of less than 3.0 as a sign of a healthy debt situation.

Howmet's substantially better financial situation is why the market values it higher than TransDigm, despite TransDigm's higher EBITDA margin.

The company has a TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 57.37, much higher than the Industrial sector median of 24.83 and its five-year average of 46.29, indicating potential overvaluation. However, more often than not, investors prefer to use forward-looking estimates when making decisions about whether to buy a stock.

The company's 2025 forward P/E to analysts' estimated earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth ("PEG") ratio is 1.51, calculated by dividing its 2025 forward P/E of 52.89 by analysts' estimated EPS growth rate of 34.90%. The market generally considers a PEG ratio of 1.0 to 2.0 as a sign of a fairly valued stock. It also considers a PEG ratio above 2.0 as overvalued. Suppose the stock traded at a 2025 PEG ratio of 2.0; its stock price would be $267.89, representing a 36.52% increase above its September 30 closing price of $196.23.

Generally, when a stock is selling at the lower end of its free cash flow ("FCF") yield, the market considers it overvalued. In contrast, when an FCF yield trades at the higher end of its range, investors think the market undervalues the stock. For a mature company like Howmet, you can make a good argument that the market overvalues the stock at an FCF yield of 1.29%.

However, another way to look at things is that investors have already factored in a high FCF growth rate over the next several years, believing it can "grow into its valuation." Currently, analysts have forecasted FCF to grow at a rate of 30% in 2025, 24.16% in 2026, and 19.69% in 2027.

The following reverse discounted cash flow ("DCF") uses a terminal growth rate of 3%, reflecting what I believe will be the U.S. market's long-term inflation rate. This reverse DCF uses a discount rate of 12.5%, which I base on Howmet Aerospace's weighted average cost of capital of 12.72%, indicating a relatively high-risk level (likely the risk of being in a cyclical industry). This reverse DCF analysis utilizes the following TTM FCF data from YCharts.

Reverse DCF

The second quarter of fiscal year ("FY") 2026 reported TTM FCF

 $1,038
Terminal growth rate 3%
Discount Rate 12.5%
Years 1-10 growth rate 31.1%
Stock Price (September 30, 2025, closing price) $196.23
Terminal FCF value $15.967 billion
Discounted Terminal Value $51.759 billion
FCF margin 31.3%

This reverse DCF analysis indicates that CrowdStrike would need to grow its FCF at a 31.3% annual rate over the next ten years to justify today's prices, a scenario unlikely for a company of its size and maturity to sustain for ten years at such a high rate. Based on this reverse DCF, the market may overvalue the stock in the long term.

Risks

Howmet operates in a highly cyclical industry. Suppose air demand declines substantially due to the global economy slowing, like it did during the pandemic; the company's revenue growth and margins may fall, and the stock could sell off. Additionally, although its 2025 PEG ratio implies an upside of 36.52%, the market may never value it at a PEG ratio of 2.0, as it may discount the stock due to its cyclicality. Therefore, the stock may have already priced in most of its earnings growth upside, and the stock price may not increase significantly from its current level. Currently, the stock has reached a 52-week high of $198.48 on October 1, 2025. As of October 1, Seeking Alpha reported that Wall Street analysts' average price target was $205.66, implying only a 5.55% upside from its October 1 close.

Howmet has a significant customer concentration. It derives a majority of its revenue from CFM International, Boeing, and Airbus. The company's sales could drop significantly if any of its primary revenue streams encounter difficulties. For instance, Boeing ran into trouble with the Federal Aviation Administration ("FAA") when it imposed production caps on the 737 MAX (Boeing) due to a series of incidents, starting with plane crashes in 2019, and the latest incident involving a door plug blowing out in early 2024. Naturally, as Boeing sold fewer planes, Howmet's OEM sales declined—a headwind for its revenue.

There are numerous other factors that could halt or delay Boeing or Airbus from manufacturing more planes, and if those risks manifest, Howmet's OEM sales could slow. Additionally, in certain situations, Boeing and Airbus may decide to burn off their spare part inventory levels rather than purchasing more parts, which could also hurt Howmet's spare parts sales.

Lastly, the company's margins are subject to inflationary factors, including rising labor and raw material costs. The aerospace industry is currently facing a severe labor shortage and is vulnerable to rising prices in vital raw materials, such as titanium and nickel. Investors typically don't react well to inflationary factors that erode a company's margins or harm sales.

Howmet is a Buy

I had a tough decision between rating this a 'buy' or 'hold,' because there is no simple answer. Everything depends on what type of investor I decide to tailor the recommendation for. If I focus on the considerations of long-term growth investors, the company's increasing annuity-like revenue mix, particularly from high-margin engine spare parts sales, may justify buying the stock today. The market may continue to raise the stock's valuation over the long term, despite its high valuation, due to its growing spare parts business. Additionally, Boeing and Airbus have multi-year, record-high aircraft backlogs for building next-generation aircraft, which provides exceptional long-term revenue visibility for Howmet's OEM business, a factor that some investors value. The company may have a line of sight on growth visibility for at least the next decade.

However, the opposing viewpoint from more cautious investors may consider the stock's near-term stretched valuation and potential limited upside and avoid it for now until it reaches a more reasonable valuation. Some more cautious investors may argue that it is a hold. As I typically favor the long-term investment perspective, I rate the stock a buy.

data.title: A 4% Yield And Big Growth Potential In Healthcare Real Estate: CareTrust REIT
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers.

CareTrust REIT, Inc. (NYSE:CTRE) is a senior-focused real estate investment trust that primarily owns skilled nursing facilities (nursing homes) but also secondarily senior living communities offering varying degrees of assistance.

It owns 408 properties in the U.S. and, after the recently closed acquisition of Care REIT plc, 137 care homes in the United Kingdom. Senior care facilities work a little differently in the UK because care homes typically act like a hybrid of assisted living and skilled nursing facilities.

(Note also that Care REIT's portfolio is of similarly high quality as that of CTRE. All properties are leased on a triple-net basis with inflation-based escalators and a weighted average remaining lease term of about 20 years.)

CTRE is paying roughly $600 million and assuming about $250 million of Care REIT's debt, which it expects to refinance in the near future.

This acquisition was completed at an estimated yield of about 8% and is expected to be accretive on a year-one basis to the tune of ~6% of normalized FFO per share.

To finance most of this deal, CTRE plans to secure a $500 million unsecured term loan during the second quarter. And just in time for that large debt raise, the REIT just earned its first-ever investment-grade credit rating of BBB- from Fitch.

To quote the upgrade report:

CTRE's ratings reflect its strong financial metrics, including very low leverage, above-average operator lease coverage and unsecured borrowing strategy. The ratings also reflect the issuer's moderately diversified portfolio of triple-net leased health care real-estate properties and long-lease maturity profile, which has become further diversified upon close of the Care REIT acquisition.

Upon closing of the acquisition, CTRE's management bumped up 2025 guidance, now estimating normalized FFO per share and FAD per share of $1.75 to $1.78.

That represents growth of about 15% from 2024's full-year FAD per share of $1.54, and analysts are projecting another low-double-digit increase in the bottom line in 2026 as well.

Fitch estimates that even after the financing of this acquisition, CTRE's debt-to-EBITDA ratio remains very low at 2.7x -- well below CTRE's own target leverage ratio of 4-5x. (CTRE estimates its pro forma net debt to EBITDA ratio post-transaction to be 2.4x.) That leaves significant room for further use of relatively low-cost debt to fund investments at huge spreads.

At a price of $34, CTRE is valued at 18.4x FAD, or about 16.5x estimated 2026 FAD.

That makes CTRE significantly cheaper than its larger and more senior housing-focused peer, Welltower (WELL), which currently trades at an FFO multiple of about 30x and an AFFO multiple of about 33x.

Moreover, CTRE's dividend yield of 4% is more than 2x higher than WELL's current dividend yield of 1.7%.

The two REITs have meaningful differences, to be sure. But importantly, while they have similar, double-digit growth rates, their valuations are worlds apart. We think the market over-appreciates WELL and under-appreciates CTRE right now.

With a skilled and shareholder-aligned management team, low-leveraged balance sheet, recent credit upgrade to investment-grade status, peer-leading cost of capital, triple-net lease structure, best-in-class tenant-operator roster, disciplined underwriting standards, reasonable valuation, and unprecedentedly strong demographic tailwinds, CTRE is very attractive as a long-term buy-and-hold investment.

Let's dive deeper into the investment thesis for CTRE.

The Long-term Case for CareTrust REIT

The first and most obvious point to note in favor of senior care real estate is the fact that populations across the developing world are aging rapidly. The U.S. and the UK are no exceptions to this trend.

In the U.S., the population of 80+ individuals (the core clientele of both assisted living and skilled nursing facilities) is projected to grow rapidly in the coming years and decades.

People are living longer and generally healthier lives, but Father Time comes for everyone, and a very large share of individuals aged 80+ require assistance with everyday living.

While in-home care has grown alongside facility care in recent years, this type of care typically requires familiar caregiver assistance as well as that of home care workers. The demographic reality, though, is that the elderly population is forecast to grow significantly faster than the middle-aged cohort of their children's generation, due to the sharp decline in the birth rate in the 1960s and 1970s.

This means that there will increasingly be no familiar caregivers available to provide in-home assistance to their elderly family members. As such, it is unlikely that in-home care will be able to eat significantly into senior housing's market share in the coming decades. (Highly dexterous humanoid robots could alter this outlook, but that's a discussion for a future date.)

On top of the strong demand tailwinds as far as the eye can see into the future, there's also some good news on the supply side.

Due to rising construction costs, the development pipeline of new senior-focused real estate has collapsed to very low levels.

Moreover, despite occupancy and rent growth in recent years, market rent rates are still not at the level necessary to catalyze new development starts in this space.

Note that all of the above is more or less true of the UK senior housing market as well.

While the senior population in the UK is growing swiftly, the number of care home beds has been flat for the last decade, allowing the beds per capita ratio to steadily slide to around its lowest point in the last 25 years.

And keep in mind that today, the demographic setup has never been better for UK care homes!

You may have noticed that we have been lumping both senior housing/assisted living and skilled nursing facilities together as "senior care real estate." That is because the demographic and supply outlook is virtually identical for both types of businesses.

But unlike senior housing, which may be lifestyle- or needs-based, skilled nursing facilities are strictly needs-based caregiving centers. They are commonly referred to as long-term care facilities. While there are some long-term acute care hospitals ("LTACHs") that provide in-patient rehabilitation for individuals after an acute event, such as a stroke or a surgery, senior-focused SNFs are basically nursing homes.

The US Department of Health & Human Services estimates that approximately 70% of individuals turning 65 today will require long-term care at some point in their lives.

Another unfortunate byproduct of longer lifespans is a greater incidence of dementia. The older a person gets, the higher the likelihood of dementia, with 10-20% of the 75-84 population and 25-50% of the 85+ population suffering from it. The presence of dementia only increases the need for specialized care.

Not counting the recent Care REIT acquisition, CTRE's portfolio is mostly made up of SNFs (59% of total revenue) but also includes multi-service campuses (also called "continuing care retirement communities") at 12% of total revenue and senior housing at 5% of total revenue. The rest comes from mortgages and other loans, almost always with purchase options included.

If you exclude interest income and look only at rental revenue from owned real estate, the picture looks a little different.

Here's how CTRE's real estate portfolio looked before and after the acquisition of Care REIT:

As you can see, after the transaction, CTRE's concentration in SNFs significantly declined as the portfolio became more diversified.

As the REIT continues to use its strong cost of capital to grow the portfolio in both the U.S. and the UK, that diversification should only continue to increase.

While worker shortages continue to be a challenge in the senior care space, it is important to note that total U.S. employment in this industry has mounted a remarkable comeback since the mass exodus of workers during the pandemic:

The highest quality operators with the greatest scale and efficiency should be able to continue attracting workers to their facilities and preventing high turnover.

While CTRE's tenancy is somewhat concentrated in its top tenant-operators, it's important to note that these are some of the best operators in the industry, led by CTRE's largest tenant, the Ensign Group (ENSG).

To quote the Fitch report from above:

As of March 31, 2025, CTRE's top five tenants represented 65% of annualized base rent (ABR), reflecting significant concentration. However, concentration has declined over time and should approach 50% pro forma for the Care REIT acquisition, aligning more closely with other health care REITs. Tenant concentration is balanced by above-average portfolio-level lease coverage. CTRE's outsized exposure to The Ensign Group, Inc. (Ensign; 27.2% of ABR in 1Q25) enhances the portfolio-level coverage ratio, with Ensign's TTM EBITDARM coverage at a high 4.25x.

The Ensign Group's rent coverage is among the strongest across the entire industry, in no small part due to its scale efficiencies and ability to attract and keep high-quality talent.

On CTRE's conference calls, management consistently reiterates their insistence upon working with best-in-class operators that provide high-quality care to their residents. This kind of quality creates a virtuous cycle in which better-managed facilities have higher occupancy rates, which in turn affords them greater ability to invest in the staffing needed to maintain high-quality controls, which then drives further occupancy gains.

The Biggest Risk Facing CareTrust REIT

Probably the biggest risk facing CTRE is the fact that its tenant-operators are heavily reliant on government funding. In the UK, Care REIT's operators derive about 2/3rds of their total revenue from the National Health Service, while the majority of U.S. operators' revenue comes from Medicare and Medicaid.

Over the years, there have been discussions on both sides of the Atlantic about cuts to these government programs that could have negative effects on the senior care industry.

For example, although the U.S. government recently implemented a 4.2% increase in reimbursement rates to SNFs for fiscal 2025, the newly enacted budget reconciliation bill—signed into law in July 2025—includes hundreds of billions of dollars in cuts to Medicaid over the next decade. This creates a clear disconnect between short-term funding relief and long-term structural risk for senior care operators.

Given that Medicaid is the primary payor for long-term care, these cuts could potentially lead to lower reimbursement rates or denial of coverage for SNF patients. This, in turn, would squeeze SNF operators' margins and likely lead to a vicious cycle of understaffing, poor quality care, and facility closures.

That said, this is the absolute worst-case scenario, and we find it highly unlikely to manifest. Governments in both the U.S. and the UK are highly likely to preserve funding for needs-based senior care, regardless of what else they decide to do with other healthcare spending.

In the U.S., the congressional budget bill does call for cuts to Medicaid, but these are only directed toward the non-senior, able-bodied population.

To quote a piece in the Wall Street Journal:

The package takes aim at Medicaid in part by instituting work requirements for most able-bodied adults through age 64 without dependents. The work requirements wouldn’t take effect until 2029—after Trump has left office. It also takes aim at California by reducing federal Medicaid payments to states that provide healthcare coverage for immigrants in the country illegally. That provision doesn’t take effect until October 2027—after the midterm elections.

The bill wouldn't even institute these work requirements until years into the future. There are several Republican lawmakers who are insistent on implementing work requirements for able-bodied adults immediately, and they may succeed in writing this into the legislation.

Even so, if Medicaid funding cuts get implemented, it remains highly unlikely that they would have any negative effect on senior care facilities.

Hospitals would likely be impacted by such cuts, but senior-focused long-term care businesses would not.

As the CEO of Omega Healthcare (OHI), a peer to CTRE, said on their Q1 2025 conference call:

We continue to believe that the Medicaid expansion population, those able-bodied adults that were added with the Affordable Care Act are likely the largest target of these spending cuts.

Given that funding cuts to the traditional Medicaid population (the disabled, single mothers and children, and the elderly) are not under consideration right now, we do not believe SNFs are likely to suffer revenue losses from public funding anytime soon.

On the flip side, high reliance on government funding can be seen as a good thing, as it makes operators' revenue streams highly stable, recession-resistant, and non-cyclical.

However, heavy reliance on government funding is something to keep an eye on going forward.

Bottom Line

CTRE is a stereotypical stalwart and steady compounder. It is both defensive and offensive in that its portfolio and balance sheet are high-quality and resilient, but also its strong cost of capital affords it the ability to invest aggressively and accretively right now.

CTRE's two direct peers in the SNF space—Omega Healthcare and Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA)—both suffer from high payout ratios and low equity valuations that prohibit stock issuance for growth purposes. Moreover, both have more tenant issues than CTRE, and neither boasts as diversified a portfolio as that of CTRE.

In fact, given its strong cost of capital, CTRE is better positioned than any other net lease senior housing/care REIT to take advantage of the silver tsunami. The demographic tailwinds have really only just begun and should continue for decades.

At about 16.5x 2026 FAD, we think CTRE could re-rate closer to a 20x multiple over time, which could mean roughly 20% further upside from here.

But even if CTRE's valuation multiple stays exactly at 16.5x going forward, we would still expect the combination of a 4% dividend yield and mid- to high-single-digit growth to generate double-digit total returns.

For investors focused on either dividend growth or total returns (or both), CTRE looks like a solid long-term bet.

We have placed it high on our watchlist at High Yield Landlord and may invest in it on a future dip.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Good Fundamentals In An Unavoidable Business
data.description:
Virtually every day, there are news telling about cybercrimes. The incidence of such events is, indeed, not only a matter of perception but a documented trend that has impacted individuals, corporations, and governments all over the globe. CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) (NEOE:CRWD:CA) is a cybersecurity player, working in the technological frontier to provide solutions to a growing number of customers. Current firm performance and growth prospects justify consideration for long-position allocations in equity portfolios from a long-term standpoint.

Cybersecurity Investment Thesis

Cybersecurity may be the fastest-growing market in the software industry. According to Morgan Stanley, the business will grow at a 50% faster pace than the rest of the software industry as a whole, with a 12% CAGR up to 2028. The underlying trend involved in this movement is unequivocal. The number of cybercrimes and threats seems to rise every day and becomes evident to everyone who follows the news. Early this month, a high-profile ransomware on the check-in and boarding system in Belgium disrupted the air traffic in Europe. Also in this month, a deepfake fraud resulted in a US$ 25 million loss at the Arup engineering firm. In August, a number of data breaches hit companies like Google, KLM–Air France, TransUnion, and others. This list goes on and on…

The current cybersecurity problem is very vast and diversified. I divide this topic into four important topics. The first is related to the enterprise problem, in which corporate systems are vulnerable to actions with the potential to disrupt operations, reputations, and impose severe financial consequences. The second dimension is the personal one. Us, individuals, with our lives connected, from social relations to personal finances, are exposed to a huge number of frauds and scams that may impose real burdens. Another topic is the risk of cyber-imposed situations involved in sovereign scale, as a result of country disputes. The current cold war character is less 007 and more “hacker.” At last, the AI rise completely reshuffles the deck. At the same time, it opens the field for newer and potentially more dangerous scams and attacks, and it also allows for enhanced security packages. The only certainty we can have is that current solutions will be replaced many times in the coming years. As a whole, in every dimension, cybersecurity is a priority, and its market potential is very strong.

In terms of the enterprise problem, 85% of CEOs recognize cybersecurity as a critical component of operations. Moreover, the cybersecurity budget increase is a response to the insurance rising-prices situation, AI, and the higher incident frequency. 72% of organizations experienced three or more types of incidents in the last year. As a result, cybersecurity now represents 13.2% of IT budgets, 4.6 p.p. higher in a five-year horizon.

On the personal dimension, the incidents are also exploding. Personal data breaches increased 186% in one quarter (Q1/2025), and phishing scams are up 466% in the same period, as well as other types of events. The scenario imposes a market opportunity for identity verification and scam and virus detectors. When thinking on the national level, cybersecurity became a central source of concern in terms of national defense. In a previous article, I noted cybersecurity as a major tech concern for the future of defense systems. From the rising geopolitical tensions and the vulnerabilities verified on basic systems such as energy supply and transportation, a robust cybersecurity spending will be embedded in rising defense budgets, including in the landscape some players like Palantir (PLTR) and General Dynamics (GD) as well.

At last, in the AI era, product development and life cycle will be completely redefined. Traditionally, products are created on a known threat library, and evolve as software developers identify additional situations. AI-developed products may be based on massive training on the patterns of attacks. In this way, cybersecurity firms will battle, besides the threats themselves, in a field of trying to consolidate the development, aiming for faster and more efficient responses to the dynamic scenario and assuring governance on the inevitable recovery events that will happen. This will be the key feature of a strategic plan for anyone in this market.

Company Position and the July 19th Incident

CrowdStrike's offer revolves around a cloud-based platform called Falcon, in which customers may add functionalities as they need. The firm does not disclose specifics about each product's commercial success, but it may seem reasonable to see the Falcon Prevent and the Falcon Insight as the most prevalent top-line sources. These solutions combine an AI-driven antivirus and malware prevention with the endpoint detection and response capabilities. On top of it, CrowdStrike also offers solutions on threat hunting, firewall, identity protection, and cloud security, besides professional services that comprise incident response.

On July 19th, a software update resulted in several incidents for clients that use Windows. The failure, initiated on the endpoint security feature, created a continuous reboot cycle that consumed computing resources and prevented systems from keeping operational. The problem impacted the transportation sector, mainly aviation, and healthcare, among others. Losses are estimated at US$ 5.4 billion. Naturally, several legal proceedings followed the issue.

Since this moment, there is a direct impact on the firm’s financials. Initially, an R&D increase resulted from the redesign of solutions and the testing environment. As an effect, last quarter alone depicted a more than 30% addition in product development. The same movement appeared in the SG&A. The company increased the budget for marketing and sales activities to recover reputational damages. According to the company, this effect, also played a role in the effort towards sales to more effectively capture market growth. Administrative expense was also impacted by the direct charges related to the crash.

After more than a year, we cannot notice a loss of momentum in the market appeal of CrowdStrike. Sales continue to improve in a quasi-linear trend, with maintenance of gross margin levels, indicating continued demand and price power. The firm continues to show premium growth compared to peers. On the other hand, Operating and Net Income registered the impact of the above-mentioned expense increases, generating a trend reversion in which margins returned to negative territory since the end of 2024. The last quarter was the first to depict a slight improvement that could signal a return to margin normality, where scale is expected to recover prominence. The continuation of this trend is crucial to the firm’s fundamental scoring, as will be explained in the next session.

Fundamental Score

In my research, I compare several target firms that are selected based on an appropriate investment thesis. Right now, some of the relevant theses include Artificial Intelligence Hardware and Applications, Infrastructure and Cybersecurity, and Defense, among others. Some comparisons in this article will be made against this broad group, and some will be made with direct competitors. CrowdStrike is to be compared against Fortinet (FTNT) and Palo Alto (PANW). Firms are compared in terms of four elements. Initially, the Cash Discipline is evaluated in terms of how well the operation converts cash. The second is related to the total yield an investor receives considering the payout and the potential reinvestment of cash at current prices. An analysis of growth components is the next category, in order to understand commercial success and how it translates into real profitability growth. The last one is a measure of momentum, as it is a strong performance indicator and also may impose an important demand engine due to the prevalence of passive investments.

As indicated in the previous session, the financial performance is directly impacted by the major incident in the middle of 2024. The impact has not been verified on the sales performance, but on the bottom line. Figure 1 depicts the historical Net Sales and Gross Margins of CrowdStrike. The firm continues to record strong sales growth and consistent margins. To be fair, the gross margins are indicating a decrease from their peak between 2023 and mid-2024, around 75%, to 73% in the last quarter. Anyway, this is aligned with peers and other software companies. On the other hand, as explained, the company faced a more challenging bottom-line scenario after the incident, resulting in negative operating and net income margins since October 2024, as can be noted in Figure 2.

On a comparative basis, Figure 3 illustrates how successful CrowdStrike has been in comparison to its peers. On a three-year basis, the firm grew at double the rate of competitors, and on a year-over-year comparison, even with the full impact of the incident, CrowdStrike still increased its top line more than 60% more robustly than the direct competition. In terms of Net Income, on the other hand, the firm is lagging. Figure 4 draws a picture of margin compression, more importantly perceived in the Net Income, in which the company consistently loses to competitors.

The current A- profitability rating reflects this exact situation, as its components are weaker as the incident impact is accounted for. For example, Net Income Margin scored a C-. On the other hand, it clearly depicts a margin power, as the firm has a 50% premium gross margin compared to the sector and almost triple the Levered Cash Flow Margin. At this point, future reports are expected to unveil improvements in the bottom line, as the incident's impact gets diluted and the firm’s response is accounted for. The median EPS estimates are in solid double digits in the report expected in April 2026 and forward.

In the balance sheet, CrowdStrike’s general financial situation improved since the acquisition of Humio in 2021, which resulted in short-term volatility in liquidity, together with a level jump in the debt levels and goodwill. In general terms, as depicted by Figure 5, there was no relevant negative impact on the liquidity, as the firm was able to keep it at a healthy level that rose to almost 2 in the last quarter. Moreover, the firm was able to properly return towards the previous debt profile, indicating the operation, including the acquired synergies, was able to fund the disbursement, as it positively generated consistent growth and margins.

In such a situation, it is observable in Figure 6 that the firm is able to generate consistent operating cash flow, both from its sales and business activities and also from a gain in internal efficiency. On the receivables side, the company’s DSO was relatively flat in the last years, with a positive variation from 98 days in 2021 to 100 in 2023 and 103 in the last annual report. The real efficiency improvement seems to be on the payables side, as the company was able to increase payment terms with suppliers, or to charge upfront fees from customers that ended up recorded as unearned sales in the balance sheet. This may be the effect of the subscription-based offer that currently accounts for the majority of CrowdStrike’s revenue. As an example, the average payables time jumped from 18 days in 2021 to 27 in 2023 and 47 in 2025.

Considering this positive operating cash performance, it is natural that the company is able to deliver also a strong number on the Free Cash Flow. As explained by Figure 7, the firm is able to generate cash at such levels that it more than funds Capex and Other Investment. Moreover, a cash increase is observed, opening the field for a continuous investment program to further boost growth and cash generation. The data in the figure is scaled by trailing 12-month sales.

Conclusion

CrowdStrike is positioned on a very strong investment thesis and has been able to capture growth and value from its product portfolio. As the cybersecurity market is expected to gain even more relevance, the firm has clear potential to keep its path that justifies extended valuation and therefore, the consideration of equity investors in terms of long-position allocation. The company seems to have overcome the operating incident, but investors should pay attention to this kind of event in the future. In addition, the firm is expected to register an improved bottom line as the situation was addressed with customers, but a delay in this recovery must also be considered a risk in a long position.

data.title: Eurozone Unemployment Ticked Up To 6.3% In August
data.description:
By Bert Colijn

The unemployment rate increased from 6.2 to 6.3% in August. While an increase in the jobless rate is never a good thing, we’d be hesitant to read too much into today’s number. The increase in the number of unemployed people was 11,000 higher than in July, but still 136,000 lower than in June. That puts this uptick into perspective.

But employment expectations have steadily weakened over recent years, which means that job growth is under pressure. In September, the Employment Expectations Index by the European Commission ticked down to the lowest level since 2021. Manufacturers are becoming less negative about hiring prospects, but the service sector saw solid job prospects falter last month.

And businesses are pushing for productivity gains as wages have increased significantly over recent years. Yet despite all of that, the labour market continues to see unemployment near all-time lows for now.

That said, the job market in the eurozone is also quite divided between the south and north. The south is seeing stronger job growth – especially in the private sector – with more structural declines in unemployment. Weaker economic growth in the north of the eurozone is also reflected in more upward pressure on unemployment. This means that while the average eurozone job market performance is quite strong, weak spots can easily be found right now.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
